===== docs/data/data-sync-and-ssot-pipeline.md =====
   1: # Data Sync And SSoT Pipeline
   2: 
   3: Parent index: [Data](./!INDEX.md)
   4: 
   5: ## Purpose
   6: 
   7: This document describes the current Space Rocks source-of-truth and data-sync pipeline.
   8: 
   9: It covers the project-wide data ownership boundaries, the active `tools/data_sync` generation workflow, generated outputs, validation commands, and the adjacent source-of-truth paths that are intentionally not owned by data-sync.
  10: 
  11: ## Overview
  12: 
  13: Space Rocks uses editable shared source files to keep client, server, player-data, and API contracts aligned.
  14: 
  15: The active `tools/data_sync` pipeline currently owns generation and drift checks for:
  16: 
  17: ```text
  18: constants
  19: packets
  20: drop_tables
  21: realtime_wire
  22: observability
  23: ```
  24: 
  25: The data-sync tool reads TOML sources under `shared/`, renders deterministic language outputs, and either writes them, diffs them, or verifies that generated outputs are current.
  26: 
  27: The broader project also has source-of-truth files that sit beside data-sync but are not generated by it. Those include OpenAPI HTTP contracts, collision-shape exports, asteroid variant data, Rails migrations, Godot scenes, and player-data physical storage schemas.
  28: 
  29: The important rule is:
  30: 
  31: ```text
  32: Edit the owning source-of-truth file.
  33: Regenerate or validate the derived outputs.
  34: Do not hand-edit generated files as authority.
  35: Do not treat one source-of-truth domain as owning another.
  36: ```
  37: 
  38: ## Source files
  39: 
  40: Active data-sync constants sources:
  41: 
  42: ```text
  43: shared/constants/server_constants.toml
  44: shared/constants/server_entities.toml
  45: shared/constants/weapons.toml
  46: shared/constants/client/presentation.toml
  47: shared/constants/client/shell.toml
  48: shared/constants/client/lobby.toml
  49: shared/constants/pickups.toml
  50: shared/constants/weapon_pickups.toml
  51: ```
  52: 
  53: Active data-sync packet sources:
  54: 
  55: ```text
  56: shared/packets/outputs.toml
  57: shared/packets/gameplay.toml
  58: shared/packets/debug.toml
  59: shared/packets/lobby.toml
  60: shared/packets/webrtc.toml
  61: shared/packets/player_data.toml
  62: ```
  63: 
  64: Physical realtime-wire source:
  65: 
  66: ```text
  67: shared/packets/realtime_wire.toml
  68: ```
  69: 
  70: Active data-sync drop-table sources:
  71: 
  72: ```text
  73: shared/drop_tables/basicasteroids.toml
  74: ```
  75: 
  76: Player-data logical schema sources validated by data-sync:
  77: 
  78: ```text
  79: shared/player_data/stats.toml
  80: shared/player_data/match_result.toml
  81: ```
  82: 
  83: Observability contract sources:
  84: 
  85: ```text
  86: shared/contracts/observability/schema.toml
  87: shared/contracts/observability/services.toml
  88: shared/contracts/observability/events.toml
  89: shared/contracts/observability/fields.toml
  90: shared/contracts/observability/redaction.toml
  91: shared/contracts/observability/retention_tiers.toml
  92: shared/contracts/observability/diagnostic_bundle.toml
  93: ```
  94: 
  95: Adjacent source-of-truth files not owned by data-sync generation:
  96: 
  97: ```text
  98: shared/contracts/http/openapi.yaml
  99: shared/collisions/collision_shapes.json
 100: shared/asteroids/variants.toml
 101: services/api-server/db/migrate/
 102: client/scenes/
 103: ```
 104: 
 105: ## Configuration
 106: 
 107: The default data-sync configuration is:
 108: 
 109: ```text
 110: tools/data_sync/config.toml
 111: ```
 112: 
 113: That configuration defines:
 114: 
 115: ```text
 116: [sot.constants]
 117: [sot.packets]
 118: [sot.drop_tables]
 119: [sot.player_data]
 120: [sot.realtime_wire]
---
 170: ## Generated outputs
 171:

 187: services/game-server/internal/game/packets.go

 191: 
 192: Realtime-wire outputs:

 199: ```
 200: 
 201: Drop-table outputs:

 206: 
 207: Observability outputs:

 213: shared/contracts/observability/generated/contract.json
 214: docs/observability/generated/contract-reference.md
 215: ```
 216: 
 217: Adjacent generated or exported outputs outside data-sync:
 218: 
 219: ```text

 221: client/scripts/generated/asteroids/asteroid_variants.gd
 222: services/game-server/internal/game/asteroids/variants.go

 224: ```
 225: 
 226: Generated files are not independent sources. If generated output is stale, update the owning source and rerun the relevant pipeline command.
 227: 
 228: ## Consumers
---
 270: ## Pipeline usage
 271: 
 272: Exactly one data-sync operation must be selected:
 273: 
 274: ```text
 275: -push
 276: -pull
 277: -diff
 278: -check
 279: -validate
 280: ```
 281: 
 282: Supported active generation domains are:
 283: 
 284: ```text
 285: -constants
 286: -packets
 287: -drop-tables
 288: -realtime-wire
 289: -observability
 290: ```
 291: 
 292: `-player_data` is currently validation-only.
 293: 
 294: Supported language flags are:
 295: 
 296: ```text
 297: -go
 298: -gds
 299: -ts
 300: -ruby
 301: ```
 302: 
 303: For `-realtime-wire`, `-go`, `-gds`, `-json`, and `-docs` select the generated outputs. For `-observability`, `-go`, `-gds`, `-ruby`, `-json`, and `-docs` select the generated outputs. `-json` and `-docs` apply to both realtime wire and observability; they are output selectors, not programming languages or general language targets for every domain.
 304: 
 305: `-push` writes generated output.
 306: 
 307: ```text
 308: data-sync -push -constants -go -gds
 309: data-sync -push -packets -go -gds
 310: data-sync -push -drop-tables -go
 311: data-sync -push -realtime-wire -go -gds -json -docs
 312: data-sync -push -observability -go -gds -ruby -json -docs
 313: ```
 314: 
 315: `-diff` renders generated output and prints a unified diff without writing.
 316: 
 317: ```text
 318: data-sync -diff -constants -go -gds
 319: data-sync -diff -packets -go -gds
 320: data-sync -diff -drop-tables -go
 321: data-sync -diff -realtime-wire -go -gds -json -docs
 322: data-sync -diff -observability -go -gds -ruby -json -docs
 323: ```
 324: 
 325: `-check` renders generated output, writes nothing, exits `0` when outputs are current, and exits `1` when generated files differ.
 326: 
 327: ```text
 328: data-sync -check -constants -go -gds
 329: data-sync -check -packets -go -gds
 330: data-sync -check -drop-tables -go
 331: data-sync -check -realtime-wire -go -gds -json -docs
 332: data-sync -check -observability -go -gds -ruby -json -docs
 333: ```
 334: 
 335: `-validate` checks configuration, source TOML structure, supported values, configured files, managed blocks, packet schemas, drop-table schemas, and player-data logical schema where requested.
 336: 
 337: ```text
 338: data-sync -validate
 339: data-sync -validate -constants
 340: data-sync -validate -packets
 341: data-sync -validate -realtime-wire
 342: data-sync -validate -drop-tables
 343: data-sync -validate -player_data
 344: data-sync -validate -observability
 345: ```
 346: 
 347: `-pull` is restricted to constants and one language at a time.
 348: 
 349: ```text
 350: data-sync -pull -constants -go
 351: data-sync -pull -constants -gds
 352: ```
 353: 
 354: Packet pull is intentionally unsupported. Packet shape changes must be made in `shared/packets/*.toml` and pushed outward.
 355: 
 356: Drop-table pull is not the active workflow. Drop-table changes should be made in `shared/drop_tables/*.toml`.
 357: 
 358: Player-data schema push/pull/diff/check is not implemented. `-player_data` is validation-only.
 359: 
 360: ## Validation commands

===== docs/data/drop-tables.md =====
   1: # Drop Tables
   2: 
   3: Parent index: [Data](./!INDEX.md)
   4: 
   5: ## Purpose
   6: 
   7: This document describes the current drop-table source-of-truth data, data-sync generation path, generated server output, runtime consumers, validation workflow, and ownership boundaries.
   8: 
   9: ## Overview
  10: 
  11: Drop tables define whether a destroyed gameplay source can produce one or more pickup results.
  12: 
  13: The current drop-table pipeline is:
  14: 
  15: ```text
  16: shared/drop_tables/basicasteroids.toml
  17: -> data-sync parses drop-table TOML
  18: -> data-sync generates Go drop-table data
  19: -> services/game-server/internal/game/drops/drop_tables.go
  20: -> Game.New installs drops.GeneratedTables
  21: -> asteroid destruction evaluates basicasteroids
  22: -> successful drop result spawns an authoritative pickup
  23: ```
  24: 
  25: Drop tables are server-authoritative. The client does not roll drop tables, choose pickup results, create authoritative pickups, or receive generated drop-table data.
  26: 
  27: The generated output is Go-only. Drop tables are separate from constants and packet schemas. They do not generate GDScript constants, packet fields, client scene data, or HTTP contracts.
  28: 
  29: The current runtime uses one configured table:
  30: 
  31: ```text
  32: table id    = basicasteroids
  33: source type = asteroid
  34: drop mode   = single
  35: ```
  36: 
  37: The current `basicasteroids` table allows at most one drop result per destroyed asteroid and caps active pickups from that table at two.
  38: 
  39: ## Source files
  40: 
---
 150: ## Configuration
 151: 
 152: Drop-table data-sync configuration is owned by:
 153: 
 154: ```text
 155: tools/data_sync/config.toml
 156: ```
 157: 
 158: The active drop-table output target is:
 159: 
 160: ```toml
 161: [drop_tables.go]
 162: files = ["services/game-server/internal/game/drops/drop_tables.go"]
 163: sections = []
 164: owns = []
 165: outputs = ["server_drop_tables"]
 166: ```
 167: 
 168: The drop-table domain only supports Go output.
 169: 
 170: The CLI accepts both spellings for the domain flag:
 171: 
 172: ```text
 173: -drop-tables
 174: -drop_tables
 175: ```
 176: 
 177: Normal project usage should prefer:
 178: 
 179: ```text
 180: -drop-tables
 181: ```
 182: 
 183: `-push`, `-diff`, and `-check` require `-go` when `-drop-tables` is selected.
 184: 
 185: Drop tables are not part of:
 186: 
 187: ```text
 188: -constants
 189: -packets
 190: -gds
 191: -ts
 192: ```
 193: 
 194: ## Generated outputs
 195: 
 196: The generated output is:
 197: 
 198: ```text
 199: services/game-server/internal/game/drops/drop_tables.go
 200: ```
 201: 
 202: The generated file contains:
 203: 
 204: ```go
 205: var GeneratedTables = Tables{
 206:     ByID: map[string]Table{
 207:         ...
 208:     },
 209: }
 210: ```
 211: 
 212: The generated Go values populate the runtime drop-table model:
 213: 
 214: ```text
 215: Tables
 216: Table
 217: Entry
 218: DropMode
 219: SourceType
 220: ```
 221: 
 222: The generated file is marked:
 223: 
 224: ```text
 225: // Code generated by tools/data_sync; DO NOT EDIT.
 226: ```
 227: 
 228: Do not hand-edit the generated file. Change `shared/drop_tables/*.toml`, then regenerate through data-sync.
 229: 
 230: ## Consumers
---
 274: ## Pipeline usage
 275: 
 276: Standard drop-table edit workflow:
 277: 
 278: ```bash
 279: data-sync -validate -drop-tables
 280: data-sync -diff -drop-tables -go
 281: data-sync -push -drop-tables -go
 282: data-sync -check -drop-tables -go
 283: ```
 284: 
 285: Use `-diff` before `-push` when reviewing generated output.
 286: 
 287: Use `-check` in verification paths to detect drift between TOML source and generated Go output.
 288: 
 289: A focused source edit flow is:
 290: 
 291: ```text
 292: 1. Edit shared/drop_tables/basicasteroids.toml.
 293: 2. Run data-sync validation.
 294: 3. Run drop-table diff.
 295: 4. Review the generated Go diff.
 296: 5. Push the generated Go output.
 297: 6. Check that generated Go output is current.
 298: 7. Run focused data-sync and game-server tests when behavior changed.
 299: ```
 300: 
 301: Adding a new drop table requires:
 302: 
 303: ```text
 304: 1. Add a new TOML file under shared/drop_tables/.
 305: 2. Give the table a unique [table].id.
 306: 3. Add the file path to [sot.drop_tables].paths in tools/data_sync/config.toml.
 307: 4. Run data-sync -diff -drop-tables -go.
 308: 5. Run data-sync -push -drop-tables -go.
 309: 6. Run data-sync -check -drop-tables -go.
 310: 7. Add or update tests for parser, generator, runtime evaluation, and game integration as needed.
 311: ```
 312: 
 313: A new table is not active in runtime merely because it is generated. Game-server runtime code must select that table from `game.dropTables.ByID`.
 314: 
 315: ## Validation commands
 316: 
 317: Data-sync commands:
 318: 
 319: ```bash
 320: data-sync -validate -drop-tables
 321: data-sync -diff -drop-tables -go
 322: data-sync -check -drop-tables -go
 323: ```
 324: 
 325: `-diff` and `-check` are the drift-enforcing commands for source-to-generated drop-table output because they load the configured drop-table source files, render the generated Go output, and compare it to the configured generated file.
 326: 
 327: Focused data-sync tests:
 328: 
 329: ```bash
 330: cd tools/data_sync
 331: python -m pytest tests/test_drop_tables_toml.py
 332: python -m pytest tests/test_drop_tables_generators.py
 333: python -m pytest tests/test_drop_tables_sync.py
 334: python -m pytest tests/test_final_flows.py
 335: ```
 336: 
 337: Focused game-server tests:
 338: 
 339: ```bash
 340: cd services/game-server
 341: go test -buildvcs=false ./internal/game/drops
 342: go test -buildvcs=false ./internal/game -run 'Drop|Pickup|AsteroidDestruction'
 343: ```
 344: 
 345: Broader game-server verification:
 346: 
 347: ```bash
 348: cd services/game-server
 349: go test -buildvcs=false ./...
 350: ```
 351: 
 352: ## Failure modes
 353: 
 354: Common drop-table pipeline failures:
 355: 
 356: ```text
 357: configured source file missing
 358: configured generated Go output missing
 359: invalid TOML syntax
 360: missing [table] block
 361: missing required table fields
 362: missing or invalid `\[\[entries\]\]` data
 363: duplicate table id across configured source files
 364: unsupported drop_mode
 365: chance outside 0.0 through 1.0
 366: max_drops_per_source lower than 1
 367: max_active_pickups lower than 0
 368: min_source_size greater than max_source_size
 369: stale generated Go output after source edits
 370: drop table generated but never selected by runtime code
 371: pickup_type generated but unknown to pickup definitions
 372: active pickup cap prevents otherwise successful drops from spawning
 373: source type mismatch returns no results
 374: source size outside all entry ranges returns no results
 375: roll values meet or exceed chance and return no results
 376: ```
 377: 
 378: Runtime no-result cases are not necessarily errors. Missing tables, source mismatches, size mismatches, failed chance rolls, and active pickup caps intentionally produce no pickup.
 379: 
 380: A generated pickup type must correspond to a server pickup definition before the game can spawn it. If the drop result names an unknown pickup type, spawn fails and no `pickup_dropped` event is recorded for that result.
 381: 
 382: ## Code or source map
 383:

===== docs/data/observability-contract.md =====
   1: # Observability Contract
   2: 
   3: Parent index: [Data](./!INDEX.md)
   4: 
   5: ## Purpose and ownership
   6: 
   7: Observability is an active `tools/data_sync` source-of-truth domain. The editable contract is the TOML source set below. `data-sync` validates the cross-file model and generates all language/runtime metadata and the generated contract reference. Runtime emitters consume generated metadata; generated outputs are never edited manually.
   8: 
   9: ## Source files
  10: 
  11: ```text
  12: shared/contracts/observability/schema.toml
  13: shared/contracts/observability/services.toml
  14: shared/contracts/observability/events.toml
  15: shared/contracts/observability/fields.toml
  16: shared/contracts/observability/redaction.toml
  17: shared/contracts/observability/retention_tiers.toml
  18: shared/contracts/observability/diagnostic_bundle.toml
  19: ```
  20: 
  21: Together these files define the envelope, service registry, event eligibility and defaults, context/free-form fields, redaction policy, retention tiers, and diagnostic-bundle contract. No service may add a local event definition or policy that changes these generated rules.
  22: 
  23: ## Generated outputs
  24: 
  25: ```text
  26: shared/go/observabilityevent/contract_generated.go
  27: client/scripts/generated/observability/contract_generated.gd
  28: services/api-server/app/lib/observability/contract_generated.rb
  29: shared/contracts/observability/generated/contract.json
  30: docs/observability/generated/contract-reference.md
  31: ```
  32: 
  33: The generated Markdown reference is a read-only rendered reference. It changes only through the observability data-sync generator.
  34: 
  35: ## Commands
  36: 
  37: From the repository root:
  38: 
  39: ```bash
  40: data-sync -validate -observability
  41: data-sync -diff -observability -go -gds -ruby -json -docs
  42: data-sync -push -observability -go -gds -ruby -json -docs
  43: data-sync -check -observability -go -gds -ruby -json -docs
  44: ```
  45: 
  46: `-validate` reads and cross-validates the source contract without writing. `-diff` renders every selected output and reports drift. `-push` writes selected generated outputs. `-check` renders without writing and fails when any selected output differs.
  47: 
  48: ## Validation rules
  49: 
  50: Validation covers:
  51: 
  52: - one valid schema/envelope definition and supported schema version;
  53: - unique service keys and emitted names;
  54: - unique event names, categories, levels, service allowlists, trace requirements, and bridge-only declarations;
  55: - context field names, types, UUID requirements, and limits;
  56: - free-form key/value/count/size limits;
  57: - redaction exact/fragment rules and rejection of ambiguous actions;
  58: - retention tier references and file policy values;
  59: - diagnostic-bundle field/event references;
  60: - deterministic output ordering and complete configured target files.
  61: 
  62: Generation fails rather than silently dropping an unsupported language or output. The Ruby selector is observability-specific; JSON and docs selectors apply to observability as well as realtime wire.
  63: 
  64: ## Generation ownership and consumers
  65: 
  66: `tools/data_sync/config.toml` owns the `[sot.observability]` source list and the `observability.go`, `observability.gds`, `observability.ruby`, `observability.json`, and `observability.docs` targets. The tool implementation and generators are the only owners of generated output content.
  67: 
  68: Consumers are:
  69: 
  70: - Go services through `shared/go/observabilityevent` and service `logging.Emit` boundaries;
  71: - the Godot client through `observability_emitter.gd` and generated GDScript constants;
  72: - API Rails through `Observability::Emitter`, `WorkerRuntime`, and `PumaHooks`;
  73: - diagnostic-aggregator through generated Go observability metadata;
  74: - handwritten service/data docs through this document and the generated contract reference.
  75: 
  76: ## Failure modes
  77: 
  78: - malformed TOML or a missing source file fails validation;
  79: - duplicate service/event/field/retention identifiers fail validation;
  80: - an event referencing an unknown service, field, or retention tier fails validation;
  81: - invalid trace/UUID, field type, redaction, or limit declarations fail validation;
  82: - unsupported selector combinations fail CLI parsing;
  83: - generated output drift causes `-check` to exit nonzero;
  84: - a runtime emitter rejects an event, unsafe value, or oversized record according to generated rejection codes;
  85: - a writer failure degrades the service-owned runtime but must not cause the workflow to raise or serialize the rejected/private payload.
  86: 
  87: The contract pipeline and runtime writer failures are separate: data-sync failure blocks a generated-contract change; a runtime write failure is a bounded service degradation.
  88: 
  89: ## Rule against manual edits
  90: 
  91: Do not edit any generated output directly. Change the owning TOML source, run validation, review the diff, push the selected outputs, and run the check command. A manual edit to a generated Go, GDScript, Ruby, JSON, or Markdown file is drift and must be overwritten by the next push.
  92: 
  93: ## Code and test map
  94: 
  95: ```text
  96: tools/data_sync/config.toml
  97: tools/data_sync/main.py
  98: tools/data_sync/data_sync/cli.py
  99: tools/data_sync/data_sync/config.py
 100: tools/data_sync/data_sync/observability_toml.py
 101: tools/data_sync/data_sync/observability_validate.py
 102: tools/data_sync/data_sync/observability_sync.py
 103: tools/data_sync/data_sync/model/observability.py
 104: tools/data_sync/data_sync/generators/observability_go.py
 105: tools/data_sync/data_sync/generators/observability_gds.py
 106: tools/data_sync/data_sync/generators/observability_ruby.py
 107: tools/data_sync/data_sync/generators/observability_json.py
 108: tools/data_sync/data_sync/generators/observability_docs.py
 109: tools/data_sync/tests/test_observability_toml.py
 110: tools/data_sync/tests/test_observability_validate.py
 111: tools/data_sync/tests/test_observability_sync.py
 112: tools/data_sync/tests/test_observability_generators.py
 113: tools/data_sync/tests/test_observability_emission_guards.py
 114: ```

===== docs/data/player-data-schema.md =====
   1: # Player Data Schema
   2: 
   3: Parent index: [Data](./!INDEX.md)
   4: 
   5: ## Purpose
   6: 
   7: This document describes the current player-data logical schema sources, the implemented contracts that mirror them, the validation pipeline, and the persistence boundaries that consume them.
   8: 
   9: It exists to keep logical player-data schema ownership separate from HTTP request/response contracts, runtime packet schemas, Rails/Postgres physical tables, embedded SQLite physical tables, and game-server simulation state.
  10: 
  11: ## Overview
  12: 
  13: Player-data schema is a logical contract for account-shaped player data. It defines the stable concepts that guest, local-profile, and authenticated-account paths expose through the player-data runtime.
  14: 
  15: The current logical schema sources are:
  16: 
  17: ```text
  18: shared/player_data/stats.toml
  19: shared/player_data/match_result.toml
  20: ```
  21: 
  22: These files define logical player-data contracts. They do not define raw SQL, Rails migrations, HTTP payloads, or generated runtime packet structs.
  23: 
  24: Current logical schema scope:
  25: 
  26: ```text
  27: Stats
  28: MatchResultSummary
  29: PlayerMatchSummary
  30: ```
  31: 
  32: Current implemented storage routes consume the logical contract differently:
  33: 
  34: | Route                 | Runtime identity        | Backing behavior                                                                |
  35: | --------------------- | ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  36: | Guest                 | `guest`                 | process-local transient memory                                                  |
  37: | Local Profile         | `local_profile`         | embedded SQLite in standard no-tag development builds                           |
  38: | Authenticated Account | `authenticated_account` | Rails/API-backed Postgres through the player-data Rails adapter when configured |
  39: 
  40: The player-data runtime owns identity-based store routing. The game server owns authoritative gameplay facts and reports resolved match results into player-data. The API server owns authenticated-account physical persistence. Embedded SQLite owns local-profile physical persistence.
  41: 
  42: ## Source files
  43: 
  44: ### `shared/player_data/stats.toml`
  45: 
  46: `stats.toml` owns the logical aggregate stats contract.
  47: 
  48: Current schema metadata:
  49: 
  50: ```text
  51: schema_name = "stats"
  52: schema_version = "v1.1"
  53: ```
  54: 
  55: Current fields:
  56: 
  57: | Field          | Type    | Default | Notes                                             |
  58: | -------------- | ------- | ------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
  59: | `total_score`  | integer | `0`     | Cumulative score across accepted match results.   |
  60: | `high_score`   | integer | `0`     | Highest single-match score seen by the aggregate. |
---
 175: ## Implemented contract mapping
 176: 
 177: ### Stats aggregation
 178: 
 179: Stores update aggregate stats from accepted match results using the same logical aggregation rules:
 180: 
 181: ```text
 182: games_played += 1
 183: total_score += score
 184: high_score = max(high_score, score)
 185: ship_deaths += ship_deaths
 186: wins += 1 when won is true and the backing route persists wins
 187: ```
 188: 
 189: Duplicate result handling is idempotent by `result_id`. Duplicate submissions return existing stats and do not apply the match result again.
 190: 
 191: ### Guest route
 192: 
 193: Guest stats use `GuestMemoryStore`.
 194: 
 195: Current behavior:
 196: 
 197: * requires `identity_kind = guest`
 198: * keeps one singleton aggregate stat object per runtime instance
 199: * tracks processed result IDs in memory
 200: * returns guest stats as found
 201: * does not persist account-shaped data
 202: * can seed a new local profile when local profile creation requests guest stat seeding
 203: 
 204: Guest stats satisfy the logical stats payload shape but are transient.
 205: 
 206: ### Local-profile route
 207: 
 208: Local-profile stats use embedded SQLite in standard no-tag development builds.
 209: 
 210: Current local SQLite tables:
 211: 
 212: ```text
 213: local_profiles
 214: local_profile_default
 215: local_player_stats
 216: local_player_match_results
 217: ```
 218: 
 219: Current local stats fields persisted in `local_player_stats`:
 220: 
 221: ```text
 222: local_profile_id
 223: total_score
 224: high_score
 225: ship_deaths
 226: games_played
 227: created_at
 228: updated_at
 229: ```
 230: 
 231: Current local match-result fields persisted in `local_player_match_results`:
 232: 
 233: ```text
 234: result_id
 235: match_id
 236: local_profile_id
 237: score
 238: ship_deaths
 239: created_at
 240: ```
 241: 
 242: Local-profile SQLite does not persist `wins`. Local-profile stat reads return the logical stats payload with `wins = 0`.
 243: 
 244: The local store also owns local profile list, create, delete, display-name update, default profile read/write, and local match-result duplicate detection.
 245: 
 246: ### Authenticated-account route
 247: 
 248: Authenticated-account stats use `RailsStore` when `PLAYER_DATA_RAILS_BASE_URL` is configured.
 249: 
 250: Current Rails read path:
 251: 
 252: ```text
 253: RailsStore.LoadStats
 254: -> POST /api/internal/player-data/stats
 255: ```
 256: 
 257: Current Rails write path:
 258: 
 259: ```text
 260: RailsStore.RecordMatchResult
 261: -> POST /internal/player-data/match-results
 262: ```
 263: 
 264: Both paths require `PLAYER_DATA_RAILS_INTERNAL_TOKEN`.
 265: 
 266: Rails/Postgres physical persistence includes:
 267: 
 268: ```text
 269: users.account_id
 270: player_stats
 271: player_match_results
 272: ```
 273: 
 274: `users.account_id` is the cross-system authenticated-account identifier. Rails `users.id` remains an internal database foreign key.
 275: 
 276: `player_stats` stores:
 277: 
 278: ```text
 279: user_id
 280: total_score
 281: high_score
 282: ship_deaths
 283: games_played
 284: wins
 285: timestamps
 286: ```
 287: 
 288: `player_match_results` stores:
 289: 
 290: ```text
 291: result_id
 292: match_id
 293: user_id
 294: score
 295: ship_deaths
 296: won
 297: timestamps
 298: ```
 299: 
 300: Rails owns the authenticated-account physical schema and persistence behavior. The logical player-data schema defines the contract those endpoints and tables must satisfy.
 301: 
 302: ## Configuration
 303: 
 304: The data-sync config includes the player-data logical schema domain:
 305: 
 306: ```text
 307: tools/data_sync/config.toml
 308: 
 309: [sot.player_data]
 310: paths = [
 311:   "shared/player_data/stats.toml",
 312:   "shared/player_data/match_result.toml",
 313: ]
 314: ```
 315: 
 316: The data-sync CLI currently supports `player_data` as a validation-only domain.
 317: 
 318: Supported:
 319: 
 320: ```text
 321: data-sync -validate -player_data
 322: ```
 323: 
 324: Not supported for `player_data`:
 325: 
 326: ```text
 327: data-sync -push -player_data ...
 328: data-sync -pull -player_data ...
 329: data-sync -diff -player_data ...
 330: data-sync -check -player_data ...
 331: ```
 332: 
 333: The CLI rejects push, pull, diff, and check for `player_data` with the current implementation.
 334: 
 335: ## Validation rules
---
 374: ## Pipeline usage
 375: 
 376: When changing only the logical player-data schema:
 377: 
 378: 1. Edit the relevant source file under `shared/player_data/`.
 379: 
 380: 2. Run:
 381: 
 382:    ```bash
 383:    data-sync -validate -player_data
 384:    ```
 385: 
 386: 3. Update mirrored implementation contracts where needed.
 387: 
 388: 4. Run the affected service tests.
 389: 
 390: When changing runtime packet payloads for player-data commands or results:
 391: 
 392: 1. Edit `shared/packets/player_data.toml`.
 393: 
 394: 2. Run:
 395: 
 396:    ```bash
 397:    data-sync -validate -packets
 398:    data-sync -diff -packets -go -gds
 399:    data-sync -push -packets -go -gds
 400:    data-sync -check -packets -go -gds
 401:    ```
 402: 
 403: 3. Review generated changes in `services/player-data/protocol/packets.go`.
 404: 
 405: 4. Run player-data runtime tests and any affected game-server integration tests.
 406: 
 407: When changing HTTP payloads:
 408: 
 409: 1. Edit `shared/contracts/http/openapi.yaml`.
 410: 2. Update Rails or hosted Go HTTP handlers as needed.
 411: 3. Run OpenAPI contract tests and affected service tests.
 412: 
 413: When changing physical storage:
 414: 
 415: 1. Update the owning physical schema:
 416: 
 417:    * Rails/Postgres: Rails migrations under `services/api-server/db/migrate/`
 418:    * Local SQLite: schema initialization in `services/player-data/playerdata/embeddedsqlite/sqlite_store.go`
 419: 2. Ensure the physical schema still satisfies the logical player-data contract.
 420: 3. Run the owning store and API tests.
 421: 
 422: ## Consumers
 423: 
 424: Current consumers of the logical player-data schema include:
 425: 
 426: * `services/player-data/playerdata/` runtime and store-routing code
 427: * `services/player-data/protocol/packets.go` packet structs that mirror stats payloads
 428: * `services/player-data/playerdata/embeddedsqlite/sqlite_store.go` local-profile persistence
 429: * `services/player-data/playerdata/rails_store.go` authenticated-account Rails adapter
 430: * `services/player-data/httpapi/` hosted HTTP handlers
 431: * `services/game-server/internal/playerdata/` match-summary types and winner resolution
 432: * `services/game-server/internal/matchreporting/` match-result command mapping
 433: * `services/api-server/app/controllers/api/internal/player_data/` internal stats read endpoint
 434: * `services/api-server/app/controllers/internal/player_data/` internal match-result write endpoint
 435: * `services/api-server/app/services/player_stats/` Rails stat serialization and mutation
 436: * `services/api-server/db/migrate/` Rails/Postgres physical schema
 437: * client profile and match-result presentation through HTTP/runtime outputs
 438: 
 439: ## Failure modes
 440: 
 441: Common schema and contract failure modes:
 442: 
 443: * `shared/player_data/*.toml` changes without corresponding runtime, store, or API updates.
 444: * Logical schema fields diverge from `shared/packets/player_data.toml`.
 445: * `services/player-data/protocol/packets.go` becomes stale after packet schema edits.
 446: * Rails serializers omit or rename logical stats fields.
 447: * Rails migrations satisfy Rails locally but no longer satisfy the shared logical contract.
 448: * Embedded SQLite stores a different local stats shape than the runtime returns.
 449: * Local-profile persistence accidentally starts treating `wins` as durable local-profile state without a schema decision.
 450: * HTTP OpenAPI changes are mistaken for logical schema changes.
 451: * Physical database tables are treated as the cross-service source of truth.
 452: * `data-sync -validate` is run without `-player_data`, leaving player-data logical schema validation out of the requested domain set.
 453:

===== docs/devtools/api-server/local-auth-smoke-flow.md =====
   1: # Local Auth Smoke Flow
   2: 
   3: Parent index: [API Server](./!INDEX.md)
   4: 
   5: ## Purpose
   6: 
   7: This document describes the local development smoke flow for API-server authentication.
   8: 
   9: It covers the development-only verification path for health, email/password auth, bearer-token current-user lookup, logout, direct Discord OAuth start, and the browser-assisted Discord login-session handoff used by the Godot client.
  10: 
  11: ## Overview
  12: 
  13: The local auth smoke flow exercises real API-server runtime endpoints. It is not a separate debug auth system and does not bypass Rails auth authority.
  14: 
  15: The smoke flow has three useful paths:
  16: 
  17: ```text
  18: Email/password API smoke
  19: = health -> register or login -> me -> logout -> me should fail
  20: 
  21: Direct Discord browser smoke
  22: = discord/start -> Discord redirect -> callback -> auth response
  23: 
  24: Godot Discord handoff smoke
  25: = login_sessions create -> browser login_url -> callback authenticates session -> exchange returns bearer token
  26: ```
  27: 
  28: Email/password smoke is the fastest check for local bearer-token lifecycle. Discord smoke verifies provider configuration, OAuth state handling, browser redirect behavior, provider profile resolution, login-session handoff, and token exchange.
  29: 
  30: Both email/password auth and Discord OAuth issue the same Space Rocks opaque bearer token shape. Rails stores only the token digest, returns the raw token once on issue, and verifies later bearer requests through `GET /api/auth/me`.
  31: 
  32: ## Debug-only scope
  33: 
  34: The smoke flow is a local development procedure. It observes and drives real API-server endpoints with normal HTTP clients such as Bruno, curl, a browser, or the Godot client.
  35: 
  36: It does not create debug-only auth routes, seed privileged users, skip password validation, bypass OAuth state checks, bypass login-session poll-secret checks, expose token digests, or give the client access to Discord secrets.
  37: 
  38: The only devtools-specific part is the procedure and tooling used to exercise the surface locally. Auth authority remains in `services/api-server/`.
  39: 
  40: ## Server authority
  41: 
  42: The API server owns:
  43: 
  44: * Rails users.
  45: * Password credentials.
  46: * Discord provider identities.
  47: * OAuth state records.
  48: * OAuth login-session records.
  49: * Opaque Space Rocks bearer-token issue, digest storage, verification, expiry, and revocation.
  50: * `GET /api/auth/me` current-user lookup.
  51: * `DELETE /api/auth/logout` token revocation.
  52: * `POST /internal/auth/verify-token` internal token verification for the Go game-server.
  53: 
  54: Smoke tools only call the API surface. They do not own authentication state.
  55: 
  56: The direct Discord browser path returns the normal auth response from the callback when the OAuth state is not attached to a login session. The Godot handoff path attaches the OAuth state to an `OauthLoginSession`; the callback authenticates that session and returns a short browser message, then the client exchanges `login_session_id` plus `poll_secret` for the normal auth response.
  57: 
  58: ## Client presentation
  59: 
  60: Bruno and curl present raw HTTP status codes, headers, and response bodies. They are useful for verifying that the API-server auth surface works before involving the Godot UI.
  61: 
  62: The browser presents the Discord login and authorization flow. After a successful Godot handoff callback, Rails returns:
  63: 
  64: ```json
  65: { "message": "You can return to the game." }
  66: ```
  67: 
  68: The Godot client presents signed-in state after it exchanges the login session for a Space Rocks bearer token, stores the token locally, validates it through `GET /api/auth/me`, and updates the main menu.
  69: 
  70: Current Godot sign-in presentation is Discord-oriented. Email/password endpoints exist at the API level and are useful for smoke testing, but the current client sign-in UI does not expose manual email/password sign-in as the normal player-facing path.
  71: 
  72: ## Commands or controls
  73: 
  74: Run the API server locally before using the smoke flow:
  75: 
  76: ```bash
  77: cd services/api-server
  78: set -a && source ../../.env && set +a
  79: bundle exec rails db:migrate
  80: bundle exec rails server
  81: ```
  82: 
  83: For test setup, keep the test database prepared:
  84: 
  85: ```bash
  86: cd services/api-server
  87: RAILS_ENV=test bundle exec rails db:test:prepare
  88: bundle exec rails test
  89: ```
  90: 
  91: Discord OAuth also requires the local Discord environment to be loaded before Rails starts:
  92: 
  93: ```text
  94: DISCORD_CLIENT_ID
  95: DISCORD_CLIENT_SECRET
  96: DISCORD_REDIRECT_URI
  97: GAME_SERVER_INTERNAL_TOKEN
  98: ```
  99: 
 100: Do not commit real Discord client secrets, OAuth codes, access tokens, Space Rocks bearer tokens, login-session poll secrets, or internal service tokens.
 101: 
 102: ## Bruno smoke flow
 103: 
 104: The Bruno collection root is:
 105: 
 106: ```text
 107: bruno-api/
 108: ```
 109: 
 110: The local Bruno environment is:
 111: 
 112: ```text
 113: bruno-api/environments/local.yml
 114: ```
 115:

===== docs/devtools/client/hitbox-overlays.md =====
   1: # Hitbox Overlays
   2: 
   3: Parent index: [Client](./!INDEX.md)
   4: 
   5: ## Purpose
   6: 
   7: This document describes the current client-side server hitbox overlay implementation.
   8: 
   9: It covers how the Godot client presents server-derived collision outlines for development diagnostics, how the overlay is controlled, what data feeds it, and where the client boundary ends.
  10: 
  11: ## Overview
  12: 
  13: The server hitbox overlay is a devtools-only visual outline layer for inspecting authoritative server collision geometry against client presentation.
  14: 
  15: The overlay is mounted in the main game scene:
  16: 
  17: ```text
  18: client/scenes/game.tscn
  19: -> ServerHitboxOverlay
  20: ```
  21: 
  22: The mounted scene is:
  23: 
  24: ```text
  25: client/scenes/devtools/server_hitbox_overlay.tscn
  26: ```
  27: 
  28: The overlay is hidden by default. It is enabled through the devtools window checkbox labeled:
  29: 
  30: ```text
  31: Show Server Collision Telemetry
  32: ```
  33: 
  34: When enabled, the client draws outline polylines for supported server-state entities using:
  35: 
  36: * the latest applied world-lane state
  37: * the latest server-provided debug shape catalog
  38: * the current `WorldSync` server-to-visual coordinate conversion
  39: 
  40: The overlay currently supports draw entries for:
  41: 
  42: * players
  43: * asteroids
  44: * bullets
  45: * pickups
  46: 
  47: The overlay does not perform collision detection. It does not decide hit results, damage, pickup collection, despawn, scoring, targeting, or gameplay validity. It is a presentation-only diagnostic view over server-owned facts.
  48: 
  49: ## Debug-only scope
  50: 
  51: The hitbox overlay is a client devtools surface.
  52: 
  53: It is not:
  54: 
  55: * player-facing HUD
  56: * production gameplay UI
  57: * a gameplay collision system
  58: * a local prediction system
  59: * a replacement for server collision bodies
  60: * a packet command surface
  61: 
  62: The overlay exists to help compare rendered client entities against server collision geometry during development.
  63: 
  64: Normal gameplay entities do not draw their own debug outlines. The visible outline layer lives under the devtools scene and script path, while normal world/runtime code only provides the read-only state and coordinate conversion needed to render the diagnostic overlay.
  65: 
  66: ## Server authority
  67: 
  68: The server remains authoritative for collision geometry and collision consequences.
  69: 
  70: The server sends a `debug_shape_catalog` packet when the session enters an eligible room state and server devtools are enabled. The packet contains reusable shape definitions, not live entity positions.
  71: 
  72: The server-side shape catalog is built from the loaded collision shape catalog:
  73: 
  74: ```text
  75: shared/collisions/collision_shapes.json
  76: -> services/game-server/internal/game/physics
  77: -> services/game-server/internal/devtools.BuildShapeCatalog
  78: -> debug_shape_catalog packet
  79: ```
  80: 
  81: The `debug_shape_catalog` packet includes shape definitions keyed by debug shape id. Each shape definition contains:
  82: 
  83: ```text
  84: id
  85: kind
  86: shape_type
  87: points
  88: ```
  89: 
  90: The shape catalog does not contain live players, asteroids, bullets, pickups, collision results, or debug collision bodies. Live entity placement comes from accumulated realtime lane state.
  91: 
  92: The client combines the shape catalog with applied world-lane state for display only.
  93: 
  94: ## Client presentation
  95: 
  96: `DevtoolsServerHitboxOverlay` owns the actual drawing.
  97: 
  98: Its draw input is an array of dictionaries with:
  99: 
 100: ```text
 101: kind
 102: id
 103: points
 104: ```
 105: 
 106: The overlay only draws entries when it is visible. Disabling the overlay clears cached entries and queues a redraw.
 107: 
 108: The overlay draws each valid `PackedVector2Array` as a closed polyline. Invalid entries, missing point arrays, empty outlines, and malformed point data are ignored.
 109: 
 110: The overlay has a high `z_index` so outlines render above normal world presentation.
 111: 
 112: ## Shape resolution
 113: 
 114: `ServerHitboxOverlayFlow` resolves shape ids from the current devtools gameplay readmodel built from lane-applied world records.
 115: 
 116: Current client shape id rules:
 117: 
 118: ```text
 119: player -> player:<ship_type>
 120: asteroid -> asteroid:<variant>
 121: bullet -> bullet
 122: pickup -> pickup:<type>
 123: ```
 124: 
 125: Player shape resolution falls back to:
 126: 
 127: ```text
 128: player:v_wing
 129: ```
 130: 
 131: Asteroid shape resolution falls back to:
 132: 
 133: ```text
 134: asteroid:0
 135: ```
 136: 
 137: if the requested asteroid variant shape is missing.
 138: 
 139: If no matching shape definition is available, the entity is skipped for overlay drawing. Missing shape data does not affect gameplay state application or normal rendering.
 140: 
 141: ## Data flow
 142: 
 143: The runtime flow is:
 144: 
 145: ```text
 146: server loads collision shape catalog
 147: -> server builds debug shape catalog packet
 148: -> client routes debug_shape_catalog packet
 149: -> ServerHitboxOverlayFlow stores shape definitions
 150: -> client receives lane-native gameplay output
 151: -> RealtimeRouter applies lane state
 152: -> DevtoolsLaneStateAdapter.build_state(...)
 153: -> GameplayComposition.apply_devtools_gameplay_state(devtools_state)
 154: -> ServerHitboxOverlayFlow stores latest devtools gameplay readmodel
 155: -> GameplayProcessFlow ticks overlay flow
 156: -> overlay flow resolves entity shape ids
 157: -> overlay flow transforms local shape points by entity position, rotation, and scale
 158: -> WorldSync converts server positions to visual positions
 159: -> DevtoolsServerHitboxOverlay draws closed outlines
 160: ```
 161: 
 162: The overlay uses applied world-lane fields after client lane application:
 163: 
 164: ```text
 165: server_players
 166: server_asteroids
 167: server_bullets
 168: server_pickups
 169: ```
 170: 
 171: It does not read live Godot entity collision nodes.
 172: 
 173: ## Commands and controls
 174: 
 175: The overlay is controlled through the devtools window, not through a direct numbered dev toggle.
 176: 
 177: The control path is:
 178: 
 179: ```text
 180: DevtoolsWindow.ShowServerHitboxesCheckBox
 181: -> show_server_hitboxes_changed
 182: -> DevtoolsWindowController
 183: -> DevtoolsWindowActionContext
 184: -> DevtoolsOverlayContext.set_server_hitboxes_enabled()
 185: -> DevtoolsServerHitboxOverlay.set_enabled()
 186: ```
 187: 
 188: The checkbox state is cached by `DevtoolsWindowController` so the UI can restore the current selected state when the devtools window is recreated.
 189: 
 190: The overlay does not send a request packet when toggled. The client only changes local visibility. The server shape catalog is sent as part of server outbound devtools presentation when eligible.
 191: 
 192: ## Build and runtime gates
 193: 
 194: Client-side gates:
 195: 
 196: * `client/scripts/devtools/dev_tools_build_flags.gd` erases numbered dev-toggle input actions when `public_build` is true.
 197: * The overlay scene is present in `game.tscn`, but starts hidden.
 198: * The overlay only draws when explicitly enabled.
 199: * The devtools window is the current UI control for toggling the overlay.
 200: 
 201: Server-side gates:
 202: 
 203: * `services/game-server/internal/devtools/enabled_default.go` enables devtools in default builds.
 204: * `services/game-server/internal/devtools/enabled_nodevtools.go` disables devtools when the server is built with the `nodevtools` tag.
 205: * `outbound.CanSendDebugShapeCatalog` only allows the shape catalog when devtools are enabled and the room is `InGame` or `GameOver`.
 206: * `writeDebugShapeCatalogMessage` tracks the last room id and sends the catalog once per room per websocket write loop.
 207: 
 208: ## Relationship to WorldSync
 209: 
 210: The overlay relies on `WorldSync` for coordinate conversion.
 211: 
 212: `ServerHitboxOverlayFlow` builds outline points in server-space first. It then calls:
 213: 
 214: ```text
 215: world_sync.visual_position_for_server_position(server_position)
 216: ```
 217: 
 218: for each transformed point.
 219: 
 220: This keeps overlay drawing aligned with the same ViewAnchor and toroidal visual coordinate basis used by normal world presentation. The overlay does not own camera anchoring, toroidal wrapping, interpolation, or render-origin policy.
 221: 
 222: ## Reset behavior
 223: 
 224: The overlay flow resets when gameplay composition resets.
 225: 
 226: Reset clears:
 227: 
 228: * latest debug collision body cache
 229: * latest devtools gameplay readmodel
 230: * stored shape catalog entries
 231: * visible overlay draw entries
 232: 
 233: The overlay itself clears entries when disabled.
 234: 
 235: This prevents stale outlines from persisting across sessions, room transitions, or gameplay resets.
 236: 
 237: ## Code map
 238: 
 239: Primary client files:
 240: 
---

 238:

 242: client/scenes/devtools/server_hitbox_overlay.tscn
 243: client/scenes/devtools/devtools_window.tscn
 244: client/scenes/game.tscn
 245: client/scripts/devtools/hitboxes/devtools_server_hitbox_overlay.gd
 246: client/scripts/devtools/hitboxes/debug_shape_catalog_packet_reader.gd
 247: client/scripts/devtools/hitboxes/debug_shape_catalog_store.gd
 248: client/scripts/devtools/hitboxes/debug_shape_id_resolver.gd

 301: 
 302: ## Tests and verification
 303: 
 304: Client tests verify that `ServerHitboxOverlayFlow`:
 305: 
 306: * draws a player outline when both gameplay state and matching catalog shape data exist
 307: * produces no draw entries when catalog shape data is missing
 308: 
 309: Client gameplay composition tests verify that the server hitbox overlay flow is reset with gameplay composition reset.
 310: 
 311: Server tests verify that `BuildShapeCatalog`:
 312: 
 313: * includes expected player, asteroid, bullet, and pickup shape ids
 314: * skips invalid shapes
 315: * returns outline points rather than origin-only placeholders
 316: 
 317: Server outbound tests verify that `debug_shape_catalog` responses:
 318: 
 319: * encode as JSON
 320: * include a non-empty `shapes` object
 321: * do not include live entity collections
 322: * do not include `debug_collision_bodies`

===== docs/devtools/client/target-and-placement-debugging.md =====
   1: # Target And Placement Debugging
   2: 
   3: Parent index: [Client](./!INDEX.md)
   4: 
   5: ## Purpose
   6: 
   7: This document describes client-side devtools for inspecting gameplay targets and performing click-based placement actions.
   8: 
   9: It covers client presentation, input routing, target read models, placement request/result forwarding, and client build/runtime gates. Server-side command execution and authoritative target validation are referenced only as authority boundaries.
  10: 
  11: ## Overview
  12: 
  13: Target and placement debugging lets a developer inspect the current canonical gameplay target, route player-only debug commands through safe target resolution, and place debug entities into the world with mouse input.
  14: 
  15: The client owns:
  16: 
  17: * Opening and refreshing the devtools window.
  18: * Displaying local player and target readouts.
  19: * Resolving devtools UI selections into command target context.
  20: * Starting temporary placement actions from hotkeys or window buttons.
  21: * Translating click or click-drag placement into devtools packets.
  22: * Preventing active placement from falling through into normal target selection.
  23: 
  24: The server owns:
  25: 
  26: * Whether a requested target is valid.
  27: * Whether a placed entity may be spawned.
  28: * The resulting gameplay mutation.
  29: * The authoritative state that confirms any target or placement outcome.
  30: 
  31: The important boundary is that client devtools may request and display debug behavior, but they do not become gameplay authority.
  32: 
  33: ## Debug-only scope
  34: 
  35: Target and placement debugging is development tooling. It is not player-facing HUD behavior and not normal gameplay UI.
  36: 
  37: The debug-only surface includes:
  38: 
  39: * Devtools window target selectors.
  40: * Local player telemetry readouts.
  41: * Target telemetry readouts.
  42: * Game Target rows in player-only command selectors.
  43: * All Players scope rows in supported player command selectors.
  44: * Spawn player, asteroid, bullet, and pickup placement requests.
  45: * Continuous bullet stream placement requests.
  46: * Server hitbox visibility controls when used alongside targeting inspection.
  47: 
  48: This tooling must not introduce a parallel gameplay system. Debug actions that mutate gameplay still route to server devtools command handling and then through the server-owned gameplay seams.
  49: 
  50: ## Server authority
  51: 
  52: Target debugging uses canonical server-owned target state.
  53: 
  54: The generic gameplay target identity is:
  55: 
  56: ```text
  57: target_kind
  58: target_id
  59: ```
  60: 
  61: For player targets, the canonical values are:
  62: 
  63: ```text
  64: target_kind = "player"
  65: target_id = <player id>
  66: ```
  67: 
  68: The client may request a target from local click state, but the request is not authoritative. The server validates the selected target kind, target ID, click position, and current target body before storing target state. The client then reads the confirmed target back through authoritative lane-applied gameplay state. Player target fields are read from active ship/world state; non-player target bodies are read from their entity-family readback.
  69: 
  70: Placement debugging also remains server-authoritative. The client sends a requested entity type, position, direction metadata, and optional player target context. The server decides whether the request applies and mutates the authoritative game state. The client observes the result through family-specific realtime readback: world lane state for ships and pickups, asteroid lifecycle/hot lanes for asteroids, and bullet lifecycle/hot lanes for projectiles.
  71: 
  72: `target_player_id` is a quarantined compatibility field for debug/player-only command paths. It is not the generic gameplay target model and should not be used for normal gameplay targeting, target readouts, or new target-capable gameplay systems.
  73: 
  74: ## Client presentation
  75: 
  76: The devtools window presents target and placement debugging through ordinary controls and raw readouts.
  77: 
  78: Target presentation includes:
  79: 
  80: * A Game Target selector showing the current canonical target.
  81: * Local player telemetry.
  82: * Target telemetry.
  83: * Player command selectors that may include All Players.
  84: * Player command selectors that may include Game Target only when the canonical target is a player.
  85: 
  86: Target telemetry displays `target_kind` and `target_id` before the raw target state dictionary. Local and target telemetry can read from either active entity state or player session state, depending on the selected source.
  87: 
  88: Placement presentation is intentionally minimal. The current implementation does not maintain a separate placement marker or preview overlay. Placement is a temporary input mode: press or drag in the world, emit a placement result, send a devtools packet, then return to normal input handling.
  89: 
  90: ## Target read models
  91: 
  92: Client devtools target read models are built from a transient devtools gameplay readmodel assembled from lane-applied world/session/overlay state.
  93: 
  94: `DevtoolsPlayerTargetModel` reads:
  95: 
  96: ```text
  97: self_id
  98: server_players
  99: player_sessions
 100: server_asteroids
 101: server_bullets
 102: server_pickups
 103: server_enemies / enemies
 104: player_lifecycle
 105: debug_statuses
 106: ```
 107: 
 108: The local player's canonical target is read from the local player's synced active state:
 109: 
 110: ```text
 111: server_players[self_id].target_kind
 112: server_players[self_id].target_id
 113: ```
 114: 
 115: If those generic fields are empty and legacy `target_player_id` is present, the model treats it as a compatibility player target for readback. New code should keep the generic fields as the primary model.
 116: 
 117: Target rows are built differently depending on command type:
 118: 
 119: * Raw target rows combine active player state and lifecycle state.
 120: * Player-only command rows may include All Players first.
 121: * Player-only command rows may include Game Target only when the canonical target is a player.
 122: * Non-player canonical targets do not resolve into player-only command rows.
 123: * Respawn rows include lifecycle-aware player rows.
 124: * Feature rows show debug feature state such as `Active` or `Inactive`.
 125: 
 126: Target state lookup depends on the selected telemetry source:
 127: 
 128: ```text
 129: world lane ship records
 130: = active player, asteroid, bullet, pickup, or enemy readback
 131: 
 132: session lane player records
 133: = durable player/session state for player targets only
 134: ```
 135: 
 136: Inactive player session state can be displayed for diagnostics, but displaying it does not make that player active, clickable, collidable, damageable, or targetable.
 137: 
 138: ## Placement flow
 139: 
 140: Placement debugging starts from either a devtools hotkey or a devtools window button.
 141: 
 142: The high-level flow is:
 143: 
 144: ```text
 145: hotkey or window button
 146: -> DevtoolsPlacementContext.request_placement_action
 147: -> configured placement route
 148: -> DevToolsSessionFlow.begin_debug_click_placement
 149: -> DebugClickPlacementFlow or DebugContinuousBulletSpawnFlow
 150: -> placement result
 151: -> DevConnectionService
 152: -> devtools packet
 153: -> server devtools command handling
 154: -> normal authoritative lane readback
 155: ```
 156: 
 157: `DevtoolsPlacementContext` refuses placement requests until gameplay state has been received. It also refuses requests when no placement route has been configured.
 158: 
 159: `DebugMouseWorldPosition` reads the current global mouse position from the gameplay scene and converts that visual position to server coordinates through world sync. Placement packets use the server position, not raw screen position.
 160: 
 161: Normal click placement records the mouse position on press and completes on release. If the release position is far enough from the press position, the client includes a normalized direction vector.
 162: 
 163: The drag threshold is:
 164: 
 165: ```text
 166: 8.0 visual units
 167: ```
 168: 
 169: Placement result fields include:
 170: 
 171: ```text
 172: action_name
 173: server_position
 174: visual_position
 175: has_direction
 176: direction
 177: target_player_id
 178: pickup_type
 179: ```
 180: 
 181: `target_player_id` is included only when the placement action was configured with a player target. `pickup_type` is included only for pickup placement.
 182: 
 183: Continuous bullet stream placement is separate from one-shot bullet placement. It requires a drag direction and sends a continuous-stream packet directly after the placement flow completes.
 184: 
 185: ## Commands or controls
 186: 
 187: The current devtools hotkey placement controls are:
 188: 
 189: ```text
 190: DevToggle6
 191: = spawn player placement
 192: 
 193: Shift + DevToggle6
 194: = spawn asteroid placement
 195: 
 196: Alt + DevToggle6
 197: = spawn bullet placement
 198: 
 199: Ctrl + Alt + DevToggle6
 200: = continuous bullet stream placement
 201: 
 202: DevToggle7
 203: = respawn local player
 204: ```
 205: 
 206: The devtools window exposes placement buttons for:
 207: 
 208: ```text
 209: Spawn Player
 210: Spawn Asteroid
---
 345: ## Relationship to real gameplay implementation areas
 346: 
 347: Target and placement debugging must reuse real gameplay and protocol seams.
 348: 
 349: Normal target selection uses the same target request path as gameplay input:
 350: 
 351: ```text
 352: TargetRequestFlow
 353: -> generated packet builder
 354: -> client connection service
 355: -> server inbound gameplay routing
 356: -> game targeting validation
 357: -> lane readback
 358: ```
 359: 
 360: Placement debugging uses devtools packets, but the server still applies the effects through server-owned game/devtools adapters. Client placement code does not directly create authoritative entities.
 361: 
 362: World position conversion also uses the normal world sync conversion path. Placement packets carry server coordinates derived from current visual position and the active world sync transform.
 363: 
 364: The client should expose only the read models needed by devtools. Rendering, collision outlines, target telemetry, and placement input belong in client devtools and gameplay input seams; authoritative mutation belongs on the server.
 365:

  39: }
  40: 
  41: func (vector Vector2) Normalized() Vector2 {
  42: 	length := vector.Length()
  43: 	if length == 0 {
  44: 		return Vector2{}
  45: 	}
  46: 
  47: 	return Vector2{
  48: 		X: vector.X / length,
  49: 		Y: vector.Y / length,
  50: 	}
  51: }
  52: 
  53: func (vector Vector2) Rotated(angle float64) Vector2 {
  54: 	cos := math.Cos(angle)
  55: 	sin := math.Sin(angle)
  56: 
  57: 	return Vector2{
  58: 		X: vector.X*cos - vector.Y*sin,
  59: 		Y: vector.X*sin + vector.Y*cos,
  60: 	}
  61: }
  62: 
  63: func (vector Vector2) LimitLength(maxLength float64) Vector2 {
  64: 	length := vector.Length()
  65: 	if length <= maxLength {
  66: 		return vector
  67: 	}
  68: 
  69: 	scale := maxLength / length
  70: 	return Vector2{
  71: 		X: vector.X * scale,
  72: 		Y: vector.Y * scale,
  73: 	}
  74: }

  11: func (game *Game) SpawnPickup(pickupType pickups.PickupType, position physics.Vector2) (*pickups.Pickup, bool, error) {
  12: 	game.mu.Lock()
  13: 	defer game.mu.Unlock()
  14: 
  15: 	return game.spawnPickupLocked(pickupType, position)
  16: }
  17: 
  18: func (game *Game) spawnPickupLocked(pickupType pickups.PickupType, position physics.Vector2) (*pickups.Pickup, bool, error) {
  19: 	definition, ok := pickups.DefinitionFor(pickupType)
  20: 	if !ok {
  21: 		return nil, false, fmt.Errorf("unknown pickup type %q", pickupType)
  22: 	}
  23: 
  24: 	game.nextPickupID++
  25: 	pickupID := fmt.Sprintf("pickup_%d", game.nextPickupID)
  26: 	pickup := &pickups.Pickup{
  27: 		ID:              pickupID,
  28: 		Type:            definition.Type,
  29: 		X:               position.X,
  30: 		Y:               position.Y,
  31: 		Health:          definition.Health,
  32: 		AgeSeconds:      0,
  33: 		LifespanSeconds: definition.LifespanSeconds,
  34: 	}
  35: 	game.entities.Pickups[pickupID] = pickup
  36: 
  37: 	return pickup, true, nil
  38: }
  39: 
  40: func (game *Game) RemovePickup(id string) bool {
  41: 	game.mu.Lock()
  42: 	defer game.mu.Unlock()
  43: 
  44: 	return game.removePickupLocked(id)
  45: }
  46: 
  47: func (game *Game) removePickupLocked(id string) bool {
  48: 	if _, ok := game.entities.Pickups[id]; !ok {
  49: 		return false
  50: 	}
  51: 
  52: 	delete(game.entities.Pickups, id)
  53: 	return true
  54: }
  55: 
  56: func (game *Game) pickupStatesLocked() map[string]runtime.PickupState {
  57: 	pickupStates := make(map[string]runtime.PickupState, len(game.entities.Pickups))
  58: 	for id, pickup := range game.entities.Pickups {
  59: 		pickupStates[id] = runtime.PickupState{
  60: 			ID:              pickup.ID,
  61: 			Type:            string(pickup.Type),
  62: 			PickupClass:     string(pickup.Class()),
  63: 			X:               pickup.X,
  64: 			Y:               pickup.Y,
  65: 			Health:          pickup.Health,
  66: 			AgeSeconds:      pickup.AgeSeconds,
  67: 			LifespanSeconds: pickup.LifespanSeconds,
  68: 		}
  69: 	}
  70: 
  71: 	return pickupStates
  72: }

   1: ## Developer Onboarding
   7: This document is the Space Rocks developer onboarding and handoff guide.
  22: tools/data_sync/        Constants, packet, and generated-output sync tooling
  23: bruno-api/              Bruno collection for local API smoke tests
  24: bruno-diagnostic-aggregator/ Bruno collection for hosted diagnostic aggregator smoke tests
  51: Godot 4.6.x       client project and GUT tests
  70: The configured main scene is:
  78: Detailed testing guidance lives in [Agent Testing Rules](./agent/testing.md). The shared CI runners are:
  82: bash tools/ci/run_go_tests.sh
  83: bash tools/ci/run_client_tests.sh
  86: GitHub Actions runs repository checks, Go tests, and Godot/GUT client tests as separate jobs. The Go runner covers player-data plus game-server default and nodevtools variants. The client job uses LFS, Godot 4.6.3, Xvfb, compatibility/software rendering, bounded stages, streamed logs, and uploaded artifacts.
  88: For server tests, assert direct mutation results for mutation semantics; use a publishing `game.Control` seam or an explicit `Game.Step` before reading presentation snapshots; and test respawn placement through `Control.SafeRespawnPosition` when placement policy is the subject. Avoidable collected skips are not acceptable; the repository gate is expected to complete with zero skips.
 102: ## Repository Shape
 104: `client/` contains the active Godot client project: scenes, scripts, assets, tests, generated client packet helpers, and generated constants.
 110: `shared/` contains shared source files used by generators and multiple runtimes, including constants, packet schemas, collision data, and related source-of-truth material.
 112: `tools/data_sync/` contains the data-sync tooling used to validate, diff, and regenerate shared outputs.
 114: `bruno-api/` contains the Bruno collection used for local API smoke testing.
 116: `bruno-diagnostic-aggregator/` contains the Bruno collection used for hosted diagnostic aggregator smoke testing.
 124: ### data-sync
 126: `data-sync` is the repo generation and synchronization tool for shared data.
 128: It is used for packet schemas, constants, generated Go outputs, generated GDScript outputs, and related shared-source validation.
 136: Use data-sync when changing shared packet or constants sources. Do not hand-edit generated outputs as the source of truth.
 138: Observability is also an active data-sync domain. Edit only `shared/contracts/observability/*.toml`, then run:
 141: data-sync -validate -observability
 142: data-sync -diff -observability -go -gds -ruby -json -docs
 143: data-sync -push -observability -go -gds -ruby -json -docs
 144: data-sync -check -observability -go -gds -ruby -json -docs
 152: data-sync -check -packets -go -gds
 153: data-sync -check -constants -go -gds
 159: data-sync -diff -packets -go -gds
 160: data-sync -diff -constants -go -gds
 166: data-sync -push -packets -go -gds
 167: data-sync -push -constants -go -gds
 170: Use `data-sync` for generated outputs. Do not use it as a replacement for understanding ownership. The owning data docs explain the source files and generated outputs.
 191: Do not manually fight generated index blocks. Manual index text belongs outside the doc-ledger marker blocks. If an index is wrong, fix the underlying file/folder placement, doc-ledger configuration, or generated description source rather than hand-editing only the generated block.
 203: Bruno is the smoke-test tool for the Rails API server and the hosted diagnostic aggregator.
 212: Use `bruno-api/` for real local API-server routes and `bruno-diagnostic-aggregator/` for hosted diagnostic aggregator checks. Keep their collections and environments separate. Bruno does not replace Rails tests, does not own HTTP contracts, does not bypass application behavior, and must not contain committed secrets.
 240: docs/devtools/api-server/bruno-smoke-tests.md
 241: docs/devtools/diagnostic-aggregator/bruno-smoke-tests.md
 276: GUT is the Godot unit test framework used by the client tests.
 278: Run client unit tests with:
 281: godot --headless --path client -s res://addons/gut/gut_cmdln.gd -gdir=res://tests/unit -ginclude_subdirs -gexit
 284: A passing GUT run can still print Godot cleanup or ObjectDB warnings. Treat the run as passing when GUT reports that all tests passed.
 286: When client file logging is enabled, log files live under the Godot user-data location, not the repository root. `user://` resolves through Godot, so the normal folder name is affected by the project/app name, and `user://logs` is only the default base dir when file output has been enabled. Use `ClientLogger.current_file_output_path()` while file output is active to see the live path. GUT/headless tests may use a different temporary Godot user-data root than normal game runs, so treat local OS paths as Godot-resolved paths rather than hard-coded repo paths.
 289: Use Godot and the EngineForge bridge to inspect scene and UI state before guessing about node paths, tree shape, scene ownership, or editor state.
 308: Run all game-server tests with:
 312: { (cd services/game-server && go test -buildvcs=false ./...); } 2>&1 | tee /dev/tty | clip.exe
 319: { (cd services/game-server && env GOCACHE=/tmp/space-rocks-go-build go test -buildvcs=false ./...); } 2>&1 | tee /dev/tty | clip.exe
 322: Do not put broad test catalogs in this onboarding doc. Use agent testing guidance and service docs for detailed test placement rules.
 342: bundle exec rails test
 348: Use Rails tests for automated API verification. Use Bruno or curl for local smoke checks.
 362: Run the client constants-boundary scan with:
 365: python3 -m pytest tools/tests/test_client_constants_boundary.py
 381: Use Air for local convenience only. A normal `go run` or `go test` path should still work without Air.
 409: For detailed MCP server usage, bridge command shape, startup commands, and troubleshooting, use:
 457: Run migrations and tests before relying on the API server after schema or auth changes:
 463: RAILS_ENV=test bundle exec rails db:test:prepare
 464: bundle exec rails test
 491: { (cd services/game-server && go test -buildvcs=false ./...); } 2>&1 | tee /dev/tty | clip.exe
 494: For Godot client unit tests:
 497: godot --headless --path client -s res://addons/gut/gut_cmdln.gd -gdir=res://tests/unit -ginclude_subdirs -gexit
 505: bundle exec rails test
 508: For packet or constants generation checks:
 511: data-sync -check -packets -go -gds
 512: data-sync -check -constants -go -gds
 518: data-sync -diff -packets -go -gds
 519: data-sync -diff -constants -go -gds
 522: For client constants-boundary checks:
 525: python3 -m pytest tools/tests/test_client_constants_boundary.py
 535: Use the detailed testing rules in:
 538: docs/agent/testing.md
 545: * [Agent](agent/!INDEX.md) - Agent workflow guidance, testing expectations, MCP usage, session memory, and implementation guardrails.
 546: * [Data](data/!INDEX.md) - Source-of-truth files, generated outputs, schemas, persistence contracts, and data-sync pipeline documentation.
 551: * [Protocol](protocol/!INDEX.md) - HTTP, WebSocket, packet, and message-flow contracts between systems.
 561: Packet fields belong in:
 564: shared/packets/*.toml
 567: Packet output routing belongs in:
 570: shared/packets/outputs.toml
 573: Constants belong in:
 576: shared/constants/
 579: Collision shapes come from the Godot export pipeline and shared collision data.
 581: Use data-sync to check, diff, and regenerate generated outputs.
 591: Devtools docs own debug-only tooling and smoke-test workflows.
 599: Do not hand-edit generated packet or constants outputs as source-of-truth changes.
 601: Do not let the client locally mutate authoritative gameplay state for normal gameplay behavior. The client should present state and send input or request packets; the server owns authoritative outcomes.
 619: Use this section only for short, high-signal warnings that help a returning developer avoid immediate mistakes.
 626: * Generated packet and constants files should be regenerated through data-sync, not hand-edited as the source.

  11: func (game *Game) SpawnPickup(pickupType pickups.PickupType, position physics.Vector2) (*pickups.Pickup, bool, error) {
  12: 	game.mu.Lock()
  13: 	defer game.mu.Unlock()
  14: 
  15: 	return game.spawnPickupLocked(pickupType, position)
  16: }
  17: 
  18: func (game *Game) spawnPickupLocked(pickupType pickups.PickupType, position physics.Vector2) (*pickups.Pickup, bool, error) {
  19: 	definition, ok := pickups.DefinitionFor(pickupType)
  20: 	if !ok {
  21: 		return nil, false, fmt.Errorf("unknown pickup type %q", pickupType)
  22: 	}
  23: 
  24: 	game.nextPickupID++
  25: 	pickupID := fmt.Sprintf("pickup_%d", game.nextPickupID)
  26: 	pickup := &pickups.Pickup{
  27: 		ID:              pickupID,
  28: 		Type:            definition.Type,
  29: 		X:               position.X,
  30: 		Y:               position.Y,
  31: 		Health:          definition.Health,
  32: 		AgeSeconds:      0,
  33: 		LifespanSeconds: definition.LifespanSeconds,
  34: 	}
  35: 	game.entities.Pickups[pickupID] = pickup
  36: 
  37: 	return pickup, true, nil
  38: }
  39: 
  40: func (game *Game) RemovePickup(id string) bool {
  41: 	game.mu.Lock()
  42: 	defer game.mu.Unlock()
  43: 
  44: 	return game.removePickupLocked(id)
  45: }
  46: 
  47: func (game *Game) removePickupLocked(id string) bool {
  48: 	if _, ok := game.entities.Pickups[id]; !ok {
  49: 		return false
  50: 	}
  51: 
  52: 	delete(game.entities.Pickups, id)
  53: 	return true
  54: }
  55: 
  56: func (game *Game) pickupStatesLocked() map[string]runtime.PickupState {
  57: 	pickupStates := make(map[string]runtime.PickupState, len(game.entities.Pickups))
  58: 	for id, pickup := range game.entities.Pickups {

  94: 	if got, want := profile.ImpactEffect.Radial.ZoneWidth, float64(constants.TorpedoRadialZoneWidth); got != want {
  95: 		t.Fatalf("ImpactEffect.Radial.ZoneWidth = %v, want %v", got, want)
  96: 	}
  97: 	if got, want := profile.ImpactEffect.Radial.ZoneSpawnSeconds, float64(constants.TorpedoRadialZoneSpawnSeconds); got != want {
  98: 		t.Fatalf("ImpactEffect.Radial.ZoneSpawnSeconds = %v, want %v", got, want)
  99: 	}
 100: 	if got, want := profile.ImpactEffect.Radial.TickSeconds, float64(constants.TorpedoRadialTickSeconds); got != want {
 101: 		t.Fatalf("ImpactEffect.Radial.TickSeconds = %v, want %v", got, want)
 102: 	}
 103: 	if got, want := profile.ImpactEffect.Radial.TotalSeconds, float64(constants.TorpedoRadialTotalSeconds); got != want {
 104: 		t.Fatalf("ImpactEffect.Radial.TotalSeconds = %v, want %v", got, want)
 105: 	}
 106: 	if got, want := profile.ImpactEffect.Radial.ZoneLifetimeSeconds, float64(constants.TorpedoRadialZoneLifetimeSeconds); got != want {
 107: 		t.Fatalf("ImpactEffect.Radial.ZoneLifetimeSeconds = %v, want %v", got, want)
 108: 	}
 109: 	if profile.ImpactEffect.Radial.Damage.Amount != int(constants.TorpedoRadialDamage) {
 110: 		t.Fatalf("ImpactEffect.Radial.Damage.Amount = %d, want %d", profile.ImpactEffect.Radial.Damage.Amount, constants.TorpedoRadialDamage)
 111: 	}
 112: 	if !profile.ImpactEffect.Radial.TargetFilter.Allows(radial.TargetAsteroid) {
 113: 		t.Fatal("expected asteroids to be allowed")
 114: 	}
 115: 	if !profile.ImpactEffect.Radial.TargetFilter.Allows(radial.TargetEnemy) {
 116: 		t.Fatal("expected enemies to be allowed")
 117: 	}
 118: 	if profile.ImpactEffect.Radial.TargetFilter.Allows(radial.TargetPlayer) {
 119: 		t.Fatal("expected players to be excluded")
 120: 	}
 121: 	if profile.ImpactEffect.Radial.TargetFilter.Allows(radial.TargetProjectile) {
 122: 		t.Fatal("expected projectiles to be excluded")
 123: 	}
 124: 	if profile.ImpactEffect.Radial.TargetFilter.Allows(radial.TargetPickup) {
 125: 		t.Fatal("expected pickups to be excluded")
 126: 	}

 140: def packet_toml(*, include_output_ids: bool = False) -> str:
 141:     go_id = 'id = "server_game_packets"\n' if include_output_ids else ""
 142:     realtime_id = 'id = "server_realtime_packets"\n' if include_output_ids else ""
 143:     gds_id = 'id = "client_packets"\n' if include_output_ids else ""
 144:     return """
 145: [[outputs]]
 146: {go_id}language = "go"
 147: path = "go/packets.go"
 148: package = "packets"
 149: packet_types = true
 150: structs = ["PlayerInputPacket"]
 151: 
 152: [[outputs]]
 153: {realtime_id}language = "go"
 154: path = "go/realtime_packets.go"
 155: package = "realtime"
 156: packet_types = true
 157: structs = []
 158: 
 159: [[outputs]]
 160: {gds_id}language = "gdscript"
 161: path = "gds/packets.gd"
 162: base = "RefCounted"
 163: builders = ["input_packet"]
 164: 
 165: [[structs]]
 166: id = "PlayerInputPacket"
 167: 
 168: [[structs.fields]]
 169: name = "type"
 170: json = "type"
 171: type = "string"
 172: 
 173: [[structs.fields]]
 174: name = "sequence"
 175: json = "sequence"
 176: type = "uint32"
 177: 
 178: [[structs.fields]]
 179: name = "shoot"
 180: json = "shoot"
 181: type = "bool"
 182: 
 183: [[structs.fields]]
 184: name = "turn"
 185: json = "turn"
 186: type = "float32"
 187: 
 188: [[packet_types]]
 189: id = "input"
 190: value = "input"
 191: 
 192: [[builders]]
 193: id = "input_packet"
 194: args = ["sequence", "shoot", "turn"]
 195: 
 196: [builders.body]
 197: type = "input"
